scenarios.name: Dot-com crash
scenarios.triggerSummary: Speculative technology valuations collapsed after years of unprofitable internet company IPOs. When the Fed raised rates in 2000, money fled growth stocks.
keyFactors: Technology and telecom stocks had Price-to-Earnings ratios of 100–200x
Many companies had zero earnings but multi-billion dollar valuations
Federal Reserve raised rates 6 times in 1999–2000
Bonds outperformed significantly — classic flight to quality
Value stocks and dividend payers held up well
scenarios.recoveryLesson: Diversification across sectors and asset classes — not just tech concentration — would have protected most of the portfolio. Bonds were a genuine hedge in this environment.

scenarios.name: Global Financial Crisis
scenarios.triggerSummary: Subprime mortgage bubble collapsed, triggering a global credit crisis. Major financial institutions failed or required government bailouts. Credit markets froze worldwide.
keyFactors: Lehman Brothers bankruptcy in September 2008 froze global credit markets
High-yield bonds and EM bonds sold off alongside equities — no safe haven in credit
Gold initially fell in September 2008 then surged as inflation fears returned
Federal Reserve cut rates to zero and launched QE1 — unprecedented at the time
TARP ($700B bank bailout) and FDIC guarantee of money market funds stabilized markets
scenarios.recoveryLesson: The GFC showed that correlations go to 1 in a genuine systemic crisis — almost everything fell together. Only US government bonds and cash truly held. Shorter time horizons before withdrawal need proportionally more protection.

scenarios.name: COVID-19 crash
scenarios.duration: 33 days to bottom — fastest bear market in history
scenarios.triggerSummary: Global pandemic forced worldwide economic shutdown. Historic speed of the crash was matched by historic speed of recovery, driven by massive fiscal and monetary stimulus.
keyFactors: Fastest 30%+ decline in S&P 500 history — just 33 days
Federal Reserve launched unlimited QE and cut rates to zero within days
$2.2 trillion CARES Act provided direct stimulus — largest in US history
Technology, biotech, and e-commerce surged in recovery as remote work accelerated
Travel, hospitality, energy, and brick-and-mortar retail devastated
whatFell: Oil and energy (also OPEC+ price war)
scenarios.recoveryLesson: Speed mattered more than depth. Investors who sold at the bottom and waited to feel safe missed one of the fastest recoveries ever. Diversification across sectors — especially technology — made recovery portfolios outperform significantly.

scenarios.name: Rate shock / inflation surge
scenarios.recoveryTime: S&P 500 recovered by late 2023; bonds partially recovered
scenarios.triggerSummary: Post-COVID inflation surge forced the Federal Reserve to raise rates from 0% to 5.25% — the fastest rate hiking cycle in 40 years. Both stocks AND bonds fell simultaneously, breaking the traditional 60/40 model.
keyFactors: Both stocks and bonds fell for the first time since 1969 — the 60/40 portfolio lost ~16%
The Fed raised rates 11 times in 12 months
Growth and tech stocks fell hardest as higher rates reduce value of future earnings
Value stocks and dividend payers significantly outperformed
Commodities (oil, gas, agriculture) surged — energy sector up 65%
TIPS and floating rate bonds were among the only fixed income safe havens
scenarios.recoveryLesson: The 2022 crisis proved that bonds are not always a hedge for equities. Portfolios with commodity exposure, TIPS, floating rate instruments, and value tilts dramatically outperformed traditional allocations. Diversification beyond stocks and bonds is now essential.
